t: Skin picking — face, arm, damaged skin areas ("acne", insect bite site).
The patient makes repeated unsuccessful attempts to stop the behavior.
Social isolation due to shame (covering hair, mouth/hands).
Hair pulling (in trichotillomania) or skin picking (in excoriation) — visible hair loss or skin damage.
The patient makes repeated attempts to stop or reduce the behavior.
Behavior causes clinically significant distress or functional impairment
Medical condition (alopecia areata, dermatological illness) or other mental disorder exception (in BDD appearance motivated; in delusion of psychotic episode; substance induced).
ICD-11 — under the common 6B25 umbrella; subspecifiers.
Clinical interview (patient often does not speak openly due to shame).
Dermatological examination — exclusion of other causes of hair loss and skin lesions (alopecia areata, tinea capitis, eczema).
Scale — Massachusetts General Hospital Hairpulling Scale (MGH-HPS), Skin Picking Scale.
Trichophagia screening — if gastrointestinal symptoms present.
Autoimmune; clear borders, "exclamation mark hairs"; no hair-pulling behavior.
Appearance-based obsession; skin picking for cosmetic purposes.
Multiple obsessions; hair and skin behavior not dominant.
Rhythmic repetitive motor; no specific interference with body.
Abdominal ultrasound in case of trichophagia.
— Awareness training, competing response (e.g., fist clenching), motivation, social support. Evidence: Bloch M.H. et al. Biol Psychiatry 2007 meta-analysis.
ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy) — targets emotional component.
Comprehensive Behavioral Treatment (ComB, Mansueto) — HRT + multimodal behavioral intervention.
N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — evidence base developing; Grant J.E. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009 RCT — efficacy in trichotillomania.
SSRI — evidence base is limited; effective in OCD, SSRI has weak response in trichotillomania; clomipramine is relatively superior.
Atypical antipsychotic adjunct — in refractory cases.
Dermatological care — management of injuries and infections.
Bloch M.H. Biol Psychiatry 2007 meta-analysis — HRT most evidence-based for trichotillomania.
Grant J.E. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009 — NAC 1200–2400 mg/day efficacy.
Habit Reversal Training (HRT — Habit Reversal Training) — Azrin (Azrin N.H.), Nunn (Nunn R.G.)
— Five core components — awareness training, competing response, motivation, social support, generalization. Gold standard in body-focused repetitive behaviors.
SCAMP model—individualized approach based on Sensor, Cognitive, Affective, Motor, Place modalities.
2. — Focus on the emotional component; Woods D.W. et al. Behav Res Ther 2006 RCT.
Glutamatergic modulator; 1200–2400 mg/day; Grant J.E. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009.
Significant improvement with HRT; treatment of comorbidity is essential.
Myth 1: "Hair pulling and skin picking are 'bad habits', not serious disorders"
Clinical impairment; risks of functional impairment, social isolation, infection, and trichobezoar (surgical).
The patient repeatedly tries and fails — this is the main symptom of the disorder; HRT or pharmacotherapy is required.
Myth 3: "SSRIs are effective for trichotillomania (like OCD)"
Response to SSRIs is lower than to TCAs; HRT is first-line; NAC adjunct; clomipramine relatively superior over SSRIs.
Myth 4: "Hiding hair (wig, makeup) is the solution"
Cosmetic camouflage short-term; primary behavioral intervention required.
Myth 5: "Only hypnosis or deep psychoanalytic therapy resolves the underlying behavioral cause"
Evidence base is limited; HRT-based behavioral approaches are superior.
Myth 6: "Skin picking is a response to 'acne' or a cosmetic problem"
Clinical impairment — patient cannot prevent behavior, creates scar and infection on skin; clinical intervention required.
WHO. ICD-11. 6B25 Body-focused repetitive behaviour disorders. 2024.
Bloch M.H. et al. Systematic review: pharmacological and behavioral treatment for trichotillomania. Biol Psychiatry 2007;62(8):839–846.
Grant J.E. et al. N-acetylcysteine, a glutamate modulator, in the treatment of trichotillomania: a double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009;66(7):756–763.
Woods D.W., Wetterneck C.T., Flessner C.A. A controlled evaluation of acceptance and commitment therapy plus habit reversal for trichotillomania. Behav Res Ther 2006;44(5):639–656.
Mansueto C.S., Goldfinger Golomb R., Thomas A.M., Stemberger R.M.T. A comprehensive model for behavioral treatment of trichotillomania. Cogn Behav Pract 1999;6(1):23–43.
